name: Place Archetypes
definition: Place Archetypes represent the universal patterns of sacred, mythic, and symbolically charged locations that appear across world mythologies and religious traditions. These archetypal places - the Cosmic Mountain, the Sacred Grove, the Underworld, the Celestial City, the Primordial Waters - serve as the stage upon which mythic dramas unfold and as symbols of psychological and spiritual states. Following Mircea Eliade's concept of sacred space, these archetypal places mark locations where the divine and human realms intersect, where ordinary geography transforms into hierophany (manifestation of the sacred). The cosmic mountain connects earth to heaven; the underworld represents death, transformation, and hidden knowledge; the garden paradise recalls lost perfection; the threshold marks passages between states of being. Understanding place archetypes illuminates how human beings have always sought to orient themselves within a meaningful cosmos, creating sacred geographies that map both outer landscape and inner experience.

keyMyths.title: Mount Olympus
keyMyths.description: In Greek mythology, Mount Olympus is the home of the Olympian gods. It represents a place of divine power, inaccessible to mortals except under exceptional circumstances, symbolizing hierarchy and the separation between the divine and mortal realms.

keyMyths.title: The Underworld
keyMyths.description: Found in various mythologies (Greek, Roman, Egyptian, etc.), the Underworld is the realm of the dead, often ruled by a god of the dead (Hades, Pluto, Osiris). It represents the inevitable fate of mortality, a place of judgment, punishment, and sometimes, transition or rebirth.

keyMyths.title: The Garden of Eden
keyMyths.description: In Abrahamic religions, the Garden of Eden is a primordial paradise, a place of innocence and abundance where humanity first lived in harmony with nature and God. Its loss symbolizes the fall of humanity, the introduction of sin and suffering, and the longing for a return to a perfect state.

keyMyths.title: Avalon
keyMyths.description: In Arthurian legend, Avalon is an island paradise, often associated with healing and immortality, where King Arthur is taken to recover after battle. It represents a sanctuary, a place of refuge, and a connection to the Otherworld, a realm beyond human comprehension.

keyMyths.title: Camelot
keyMyths.description: Camelot, in Arthurian legend, represents the ideal kingdom, a place of chivalry, justice, and order under the reign of King Arthur. Its eventual downfall symbolizes the fragility of utopian ideals and the inevitable corruption that can undermine even the most noble endeavors.

sources.name: Bachelard, Gaston. *The Poetics of Space.*
sources.description: Explores the psychological impact of spaces and places on the human imagination.
sources.title: Bachelard, Gaston. *The Poetics of Space.*

sources.title: Campbell, Joseph. *The Hero with a Thousand Faces.*
